Title: Flower colour and cytochromes P450
Journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences

Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional co-expression with petunia Hf1 F3'5'H in petunia/carnation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Petunia cytochrome b5 specifically transfers electrons to Hf1 F3'5'H. 2. Co-expression of petunia cytochrome b5 with petunia F3'5'H in carnation enhanced delphinidin accumulation and petal colour change. 3. Improved electron transfer can help introduced F3'5'H outcompete endogenous enzymes.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances efficient delphinidin production when paired with F3'5'H.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as electron-transfer support rather than direct pathway catalyst.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful accessory gene to improve F3'5'H performance in metabolic engineering of blue flowers.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports anthocyanin hydroxylation and flower colour determination
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical demonstration of electron transfer to F3'5'H
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NADPH-cytochrome P450 reductase transfers electrons from NADPH to petunia F3'5'H. 2. It is required for P450 catalytic function underlying anthocyanin hydroxylation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports F3'5'H activity and thus delphinidin formation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: General redox partner; not manipulated directly as a major engineering target in the review.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Background enabling factor for P450 engineering.

Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Use of floral-specific CHS promoters to drive transgenes
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Floral-specific CHS promoters from snapdragon and petunia were effective for petal-targeted expression of F3'5'H transgenes. 2. Promoter choice strongly influenced engineering outcomes.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirectly enabled petal-specific metabolic engineering of flower colour.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because CHS promoter sequences were repeatedly central to transgene design; CHS coding region itself was not the focus.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong floral-specific promoter resource for anthocyanin engineering.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Snapdragon CHS promoter; petunia CHS promoter

Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Use of carnation ANS promoter/terminator in transgenic constructs
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Carnation ANS promoter and terminator were used in some commercial constructs to express petunia or pansy/salvia F3'5'H transgenes. 2. Regulatory sequence choice contributed to successful flower-colour engineering.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirect support for engineered delphinidin accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ANS coding function itself was not dissected; included as a construct component relevant to metabolic engineering.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful petal-active regulatory sequence for transgene deployment in carnation.

Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene suppression combined with F3'5'H suppression
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. In gentian, aromatic acyltransferase contributes to biosynthesis of the highly decorated blue anthocyanin gentiodelphin. 2. Additional suppression of this acyltransferase together with F3'5'H suppression altered flower colour toward lilac or pale blue.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports stable blue gentian coloration through anthocyanin decoration.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a P450; highlighted because anthocyanin decoration strongly affects final colour beyond aglycone identity.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target when tuning final hue through anthocyanin modification rather than only precursor hydroxylation.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP75B family; examples include CYP75B2 (Petunia hybrida), CYP75B29 (Antirrhinum majus), CYP75B19 (Ipomoea nil), CYP75B28 (Dianthus caryophyllus)
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: B-ring hydroxylation branch determining cyanidin versus pelargonidin/delphinidin patterns
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the flavonoid B-ring at the 3' position on flavanones, dihydroflavonols, flavonols and flavones
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 3'-hydroxylated flavonoids including dihydroquercetin-type intermediates and cyanidin precursors
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Diverts flux toward cyanidin-type anthocyanins and away from pelargonidin; in combination with F3'5'H status affects delphinidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Determines flower colour and influences pollinator interactions
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme for anthocyanin B-ring hydroxylation pattern
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microsomal enzyme assay with radiolabelled flavonoids; recombinant expression in yeast; transgenic suppression/overexpression in plants; flower colour and anthocyanidin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Flavonoid biosynthetic metabolon components; cinnamic acid 4-hydroxylase; F3'5'H
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3'H determines 3'-hydroxylation pattern of anthocyanin precursors and strongly affects flower colour. 2. Loss-of-function mutations in F3'H in morning glory and soybean-associated tissues shift pigmentation toward pelargonidin-related/red phenotypes. 3. Suppression of F3'H together with compatible DFR expression is an effective strategy to engineer orange/red flowers via pelargonidin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional F3'H promotes cyanidin-type pigmentation; suppression or mutation can redirect flux toward pelargonidin and orange/red flowers.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: F3'H mostly belongs to CYP75B. In Compositae, F3'5'H can also derive from CYP75B duplication/neofunctionalization, complicating sequence-based assignment.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for downregulation/editing to obtain pelargonidin-based orange/red flowers, often requiring stacking with DFR alleles that accept DHK and sometimes FLS suppression.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suppression can increase flavonols because of competition for intermediates and may limit reacquisition of blue traits in evolutionary contexts.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP75A family; examples include petunia Hf1/CYP75A1 and Hf2/CYP75A3; pansy F3'5'H; CYP75B5/B17/B18 in Compositae neofunctionalized F3'5'Hs
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Multiple plant species including petunia, pansy, salvia, lisianthus, periwinkle, canterbury bells, gentian, butterfly pea
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Petunia; tobacco; carnation; rose; torenia; gentian; cyclamen; multiple ornamentals
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Examples: White Unesco, Unesco, Cream Cinderella, Cerise Westpearl, Kortina Chanel in carnation; multiple rose cultivars
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: B-ring trihydroxylation branch leading to delphinidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the flavonoid B-ring at both 3' and 5' positions on flavanones, dihydroflavonols, flavonols and flavones
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 3',5'-hydroxylated flavonoids including dihydromyricetin-type intermediates and delphinidin precursors
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly determines flux toward delphinidin-based anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Confers violet/blue hues and affects ornamental value and pollinator interactions
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S constitutive; floral-specific snapdragon CHS promoter; petunia F3'5'H Hf1 promoter; carnation ANS promoter; Mac-1 hybrid promoter for stacked DFR constructs
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Critical molecular tool and key branch-switch enzyme for blue/violet flower engineering
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant yeast assays; plant transformation and complementation; RNAi suppression; anthocyanidin/metabolite profiling; flower colour phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: NADPH cytochrome P450 reductase; petunia cytochrome b5; flavonoid metabolon components
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3'5'H is the major determinant of delphinidin biosynthesis and blue/violet flower colour. 2. Many commercially important flowers such as rose and carnation lack endogenous F3'5'H, and heterologous expression redirects anthocyanin flux to delphinidin. 3. Suppression of F3'5'H reduces B-ring hydroxylation and shifts colour toward cyanidin or pelargonidin-derived red/pink phenotypes. 4. Gene source strongly affects engineering success, with pansy F3'5'H particularly effective in carnation and rose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes delphinidin accumulation and novel blue/violet hues; suppression shifts flowers away from blue toward pink/red.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Most F3'5'Hs belong to CYP75A, but some Compositae F3'5'Hs are neofunctionalized CYP75B genes. Rose showed strong source-sequence dependence and transcript stability issues with some donor genes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Premier engineering target for creating blue/violet flowers in species lacking delphinidin. Best deployed with cultivar selection, promoter optimization, compatible DFR/cytochrome b5 stacking, and sometimes suppression of endogenous competing enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competition from endogenous F3'H, DFR, and FLS can reduce delphinidin purity; some stacked constructs caused growth retardation in rose.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP93B family; examples include torenia FNSII, gentian FNSII, gerbera CYP93B2, licorice CYP93B1; rice CYP93G2 for F2H-related flavone C-glucoside pathway
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Snapdragon; torenia; gerbera; gentian; multiple species
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Flavone branch from flavanones; flavone C-glucoside pathway in rice via F2H-related route
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts flavanones to flavones directly; related CYP93 enzymes in some species catalyse flavanone 2-hydroxylation as part of indirect flavone/isoflavone pathways
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Side-route branch competing for common flavanone precursors
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Produces co-pigment flavones but competes with anthocyanin precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Acts as co-pigment that can blue or darken flower colour; also involved in plant–microbe interactions
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting; important competing branch enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; heterologous expression in transgenic plants; metabolite profiling of flavones and anthocyanins; colour assessment
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FNSII produces flavones that can serve as co-pigments for anthocyanins. 2. Transgenic expression of torenia or gentian FNSII in petunia, rose, or tobacco increased flavones but reduced anthocyanins and caused paler flowers. 3. Suppression of FNSII in torenia also reduced anthocyanin levels, suggesting flavones may stabilize anthocyanins in some species.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Often causes flavone accumulation but unexpectedly paler flowers due to depletion of anthocyanin precursors; in torenia, endogenous FNSII seems required for full colour stability.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP93B family includes both direct FNSII enzymes and F2H enzymes depending on species; functional assignment requires biochemical testing.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tool to modify co-pigmentation, but should be used cautiously because precursor competition often decreases anthocyanin intensity unless pathway balance is optimized.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced anthocyanin accumulation and paler flowers are common after overexpression.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Petunia; rose; gerbera; maize; pelargonium; iris; carnation and others
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Carnation; rose; petunia; tobacco; torenia; Nierembergia; African daisy
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Examples include White Unesco, Unesco, Cream Cinderella, Cerise Westpearl, Kortina Chanel in carnation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols such as dihydrokaempferol, dihydroquercetin and dihydromyricetin to corresponding leucoanthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream downstream of hydroxylation branch choice
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for anthocyanin formation and substrate specificity constrains pelargonidin versus delphinidin/cyanidin output
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Mac-1; genomic clone without stated promoter details in some constructs; CaMV 35S in some engineering contexts
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key downstream specificity determinant in practical engineering
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; precursor feeding; transgenic complementation/overexpression; RNAi silencing; anthocyanidin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Competes functionally with FLS for dihydroflavonols
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR substrate specificity strongly influences whether pelargonidin, cyanidin, or delphinidin accumulates. 2. Petunia DFR efficiently uses dihydromyricetin but poorly uses DHK, favoring delphinidin over pelargonidin. 3. Engineering orange/red flowers often requires a heterologous DFR able to reduce DHK plus suppression of F3'H/F3'5'H and sometimes FLS. 4. Petunia DFR was essential in several commercial blue/violet carnation products stacked with F3'5'H.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Compatible DFR alleles increase desired anthocyanin branch products; inappropriate specificity blocks target colour engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a P450, but repeatedly a primary experimental gene in metabolic redirection strategies throughout the review.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical stacking partner with F3'5'H for blue/violet engineering and with F3'H/F3'5'H suppression for red/orange engineering; allele choice should match available dihydroflavonol pool.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competition with FLS may divert intermediates; some RNAi/stacking designs caused growth defects in rose.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Endogenous genes in petunia, tobacco, Nierembergia and others
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Flavonol branch competing with anthocyanin branch
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Dihydroflavonols such as DHK and DHQ
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Flavonols such as kaempferol and quercetin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with anthocyanin accumulation by draining shared dihydroflavonol precursors
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Affects flower colour intensity and co-pigmentation environment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolite changes after F3'H/F3'5'H suppression and direct downregulation in transgenic tobacco/Nierembergia
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FLS competes with DFR for dihydroflavonols. 2. Increased flavonols were observed after F3'5'H or F3'H downregulation in petunia, indicating competition for DHQ/DHK. 3. Efficient pelargonidin engineering in tobacco and Nierembergia required FLS suppression in addition to DFR expression and hydroxylase suppression.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Restricts anthocyanin accumulation and can limit pelargonidin engineering unless downregulated.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as a major competing enzyme rather than the central subject of the review, but repeatedly essential in engineering outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful co-target for editing/silencing when maximizing anthocyanins, especially pelargonidin-based colours.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing flavonols may alter co-pigmentation and other physiological roles.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Higher expression associated with increased anthocyanin pathway activity contributing to dark-red colour
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition/species-specific contributor acting with reduced F3'5'H expression to shift colour from light-blue to dark-red
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative expression analysis associated with flower-colour transition
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Transition from light-blue to dark-red in Phlox drummondii involved lower F3'5'H expression and higher expression of an R2R3-Myb regulator. 2. The Myb factor contributes to activation of the anthocyanin pathway and darker red pigmentation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher expression contributes to dark-red flower colour.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as part of a two-gene explanation rather than a deeply dissected focal regulator in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory lever to intensify anthocyanin accumulation when combined with pathway branch engineering.
